region_of_origin: Nepal / Tibet, widely adopted across South Asia and Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dumpling class (FOODON_03315671). No specific momo subclass exists; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers filled dough/pasta products including dumplings. No momo-specific code exists; closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for momo or South Asian dumplings as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of chicken momos is approximately 250 g (6–8 pieces), providing around 450 kcal, 25 g protein, 20 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken Dumpling (Steamed/Fried), closest analogous composite, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact momo entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for steamed chicken dumplings (FDC ID 2103689) and fried chicken dumplings, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for chicken mince and wheat flour components. The blended per-100g values represent a 50:50 steamed/fried composite consistent with the dish description. Input values from the original record (confidence 0.7, provenance ai_generated) were retained only where they fell within the plausible range confirmed by USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN data. The original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not contain a specific momo nutrient entry and was not used as a primary nutrient source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken momos. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale where low = ≤55, medium = 56–69, high = ≥70) based on analogous filled wheat-flour dumplings (e.g., steamed wheat dumplings, GI ~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The wheat-flour (maida) wrapper is the primary glycaemic driver; the chicken filling has negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (45 g) / 100 = 24.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 280 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 700 mg sodium, representing ~30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 3–4 pieces (approx. 120 g), choose steamed over fried preparation, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chilli sauce to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour (maida) wrapper has a low-to-medium GI (~55) but a high glycemic load per standard serving (~24.8), requiring portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to 3–4 pieces per meal, choose steamed over fried to reduce fat-driven insulin resistance, pair with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic response, and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2 g/100 g and sodium 280 mg/100 g are moderate; fried variant significantly increases total fat and caloric density.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose steamed momos exclusively to avoid the additional saturated and trans fats from deep-frying, limit portion to 3–4 pieces, and use low-sodium dipping sauces to manage combined sodium and fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying produces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; refined flour contributes to pro-inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly avoid the fried variant due to AGE formation, choose steamed preparation only, limit refined flour intake, and monitor for nightshade sensitivity from green chillies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Momos are South Asian steamed or fried dumplings originating from Nepal and Tibet, widely popular across India and adapted in Singapore's food scene. They consist of a thin all-purpose wheat flour (maida) wrapper filled with seasoned minced chicken mixed with onion, garlic, ginger, green chilli, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Steamed momos are soft, juicy, and lighter in calories (~180 kcal/100 g), while fried momos have a crispy golden exterior with higher fat content. Per 100 g, chicken momos provide approximately 180 kcal, 10 g protein, 8 g fat (2 g saturated), 18 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 280 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low by University of Sydney classification), driven primarily by the refined wheat flour wrapper, but the glycemic load per standard 250 g serving is high (~24.8), requiring portion control. Clinically, the moderate sodium content warrants caution for hypertension patients, the high glycemic load per serving requires portion control for type 2 diabetes, and the fried variant's elevated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease. Momos are typically served with a spiced tomato-chilli dipping sauce or sesame-based chutney. They are a popular street food, restaurant appetiser, and party snack across South Asian communities in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken momos?
Is momo good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact momo entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed/fried chicken dumpling entries and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. Input values from the original record (provenance ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained only where consistent with approved-source ranges. GI is an analogous estimate from wheat-flour dumpling data in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite of steamed and fried preparation methods as described in the dish name. The fried variant will have higher fat_g (estimated +4–6 g/100 g) and energy_kcal (estimated +30–50 kcal/100 g) compared to the steamed variant. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for chicken momos as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central chicken dumpling fatty acid profiles; these are approximate. 4. potassium_mg (200) is estimated from chicken, onion, and garlic component contributions per USDA FoodData Central; this is an approximation. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singapore food service preparation with halal-certified chicken; individual vendor certification should be verified. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not contain a specific momo nutrient entry; it was not used as a primary nutrient source. USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN are the approved sources used. 7. gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale (low = GI ≤55, medium = 56–69, high = ≥70); GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category.
